metrics.narrative: The buyer has articulated a clear procurement transformation pain centered on contract lifecycle management and supplier management modernization, but no quantified business case has been shared. On the 23 January 2025 intro call, Peter Stern (Director Procurement Practice) asked whether a business case was already developed, and Luke Dougherty (Zycus) indicated willingness to share supporting materials ('review the business case for the VEP partner solution and share the team's previous priorities and focus areas'). The 3 February 2026 Next Step update notes 'Very interested in health care related use cases', suggesting vertical-specific pain around clinical procurement or veterinary supply chain, but no cost, cycle-time, error-rate, or savings target has been stated. The 8 December 2025 note confirms Rob McCulloch 'has expressed interest, has pain', but the pain is not quantified. The buyer's PSM (practice management system) rollout was the primary blocker through Q1 2026, and its completion (11 May: 'PSM rollout is coming to completion') clears the path for S2C evaluation, implying the pain is real and sequenced behind another transformation, not questioned. To move to the relevant stakeholder, the next step is to co-develop a quantified value case with Rob McCulloch: target savings (% spend under management, contract compliance lift, cycle-time reduction), ROI timeline, and TCO vs. current manual/legacy state.

champion.narrative: Champion is developing but not yet proven. Rob McCulloch (General Manager Group Procurement, Decision Maker contact role) is the internal champion: he expressed interest and pain (8 Dec 2025), drove the initial PLAN event connection (28 Nov 2025), and is the named buyer contact for this opportunity. However, his advocacy is untested. He did NOT attend the 23 January 2025 intro call (Peter Stern, Director Procurement Practice, attended instead, suggesting Rob delegated early discovery to a peer or direct report). Rob has not introduced Luke to the Economic Buyer (CFO or CTO), has not brought other stakeholders into the process (single-threaded deal), and has not driven a formal RFP or demo request. The Next Step history shows Luke pursuing Rob (disco meeting attempts, onsite meeting booked, follow-up cadence), not Rob pulling Luke into the buyer's process. Rob's engagement has been passive: he confirmed timing (11 May: Q4 opportunity) but has not initiated next steps. The 31 March 2026 note ('Can't make it to Horizon, am meeting in Sydney for lunch next Month to discuss project in Q2 / Q3') shows Rob declined the Horizon event but agreed to a 1:1 lunch, a mixed signal (still engaged, but not prioritizing Zycus events). To strengthen the championship, Luke must ask Rob directly: (1) will you sponsor this internally and bring us to the CFO/CTO, (2) will you share the business case or budget envelope you are working with, and (3) will you advocate for Zycus if other vendors enter the process. Until Rob demonstrates active advocacy (introductions, internal selling, competitive blocking), he is a friendly contact, not a proven champion.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed but not quantified. Rob McCulloch (General Manager Group Procurement, Decision Maker) 'has expressed interest, has pain' (8 Dec 2025 Next Step), and the 28 November 2025 PLAN event note states 'interested in Zycus as there is a new CPO they would like to make changes and might want to implement new solutions', indicating organizational pain tied to a leadership change (new CPO driving modernization). The 3 February 2026 Next Step clarifies vertical-specific pain: 'Very interested in health care related use cases', pointing to procurement challenges unique to veterinary practices (clinical supply chain, multi-site contract compliance, supplier risk in medical/pharmaceutical categories). On the 23 January 2025 intro call, Peter Stern's questions reveal operational pain in contract management: the need to digitize scanned contracts (manual/legacy state), extract metadata at scale (OCR pain), auto-detect clauses (compliance/risk exposure), and customize workflows (rigid current process). decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's evaluation criteria are emerging but not formalized. On the 23 January 2025 intro call, Peter Stern asked detailed capability questions that reveal implicit criteria: (1) OCR and data extraction for digitizing scanned contracts ('would you be able to digitalize existing scanned contracts and then extract metadata?'), (2) AI-driven clause detection ('How many clauses can your AI auto-detect?'), (3) contract authoring and workflow customization ('we would be able to set up our own workflows when it comes to contract signing processes?'), and (4) tight module integration ('Zycus is built organically with a single line of code, ensuring tight integration across modules' — Stern validated this matters). The 3 February 2026 Next Step notes 'Very interested in health care related use cases', signaling vertical fit (veterinary/clinical procurement) is a criterion. The product scope is broad (iContract, iSource, iManage, iSave, iSupplier), suggesting the buyer values a unified platform over point solutions. However, no formal RFP, RFI, or written evaluation scorecard has been shared. The criteria are inferred from one discovery call and rep notes, not confirmed in a structured process. To move to Shortlisted, the deal team must formalize these into a written ShoeFit / BRD validation: does Zycus meet the OCR/AI clause requirements, can it handle veterinary supply chain nuances (e.g., clinical product compliance, multi-site contract rollout), and how does the integrated platform compare to best-of-breed alternatives (if any are being evaluated).

ai_fit_signal.summary: The buyer is AI Curious, leaning toward AI Hungry in the contract management domain. On the 23 January 2025 intro call, Peter Stern proactively asked about AI clause detection ('How many clauses can your AI auto-detect?'), OCR for digitizing scanned contracts, and metadata extraction — all AI/ML-driven capabilities — signaling interest in automation and intelligent document processing. The 3 February 2026 Next Step notes 'Very interested in health care related use cases', implying appetite for vertical AI application (clinical procurement, compliance, supplier risk). The buyer's pain (manual contract management, legacy/scanned documents, workflow rigidity per Stern's questions) is a natural fit for AI-driven modernization. However, no explicit AI strategy, executive AI mandate, or quantified AI ROI target has been articulated, so the signal is Curious (asking AI capability questions, open to AI use cases) rather than Hungry (demanding AI, tying it to strategic transformation). The AIS fields are null, so no Salesforce-based AI excitement score exists. The buyer is primed for an AI-led pitch (Merlin, I2O, agentic negotiation) but has not yet been sold on it as a strategic differentiator. Zycus should lead with AI in the next engagement to test and amplify this curiosity into hunger.

The buyer's stated Q4 timing aligns with the close date, but momentum has stalled for 7 weeks.
